- verify-all: Run the full check + clippy sweep across the workspace AND the WASM plugin (panopt-zellij). Use after non-trivial edits, before declaring work done, or when asked to verify the repo builds cleanly. Captures the dual-target dance (workspace builds for host, panopt-zellij builds only for wasm32-wasip1) so you don't have to remember the right manifest-path/target each time.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	This is early alpha.  You can kinda do useful things with it right now, but it's
2	lacking a bunch of functionality and is likely to see fundamental changes.
3	
4	# PANopt
5	
6	PANopt is the shared brain for a desk of terminal-based coding agents.
7	
8	One long-running daemon holds todos, scratchpads, advisory locks, agent
9	tools, and processes for every project you work in, and surfaces them as a
10	sidebar inside a [Zellij](https://zellij.dev) cockpit. Agents (Claude Code,
11	Codex, anything that speaks MCP) connect to the daemon as ordinary MCP clients.
12	They see the same todos you see in the sidebar, can hand work off to each other
13	through locks and comments, and write notes into scratchpads that you can read
14	live.
15	
16	![The PANopt cockpit: five stacked sidebar panes on the left (todos, agents, terminals, commands, scratchpads), a content pane on the right](docs/cockpit.png)
17	
18	The cockpit above is one Zellij session. The leftmost column is five
19	vertically stacked sidebar panes - one for todos, agents, terminals, commands,
20	and scratchpads - each rendered by its own instance of the PANopt plugin. The
21	right side is the content slot: selecting any sidebar item swaps that item's
22	pane into the slot, suppressing whatever was there. Agents, terminals, and
23	commands run as ordinary Zellij panes; the slot just decides which one is
24	visible.
25	
26	## Why you might want it
27	
28	- You run more than one agent at a time and you want them to coordinate
29	  instead of stepping on each other.
30	- You want a single place to see what every agent is doing, what it is stuck
31	  on, and what it has written down, without alt-tabbing through windows.
32	- You want your todos and notes to live in plain markdown files inside the
33	  project, not in someone else's cloud.
34	
35	## Prerequisites
36	
37	- [Zellij](https://zellij.dev) on your `PATH` (the cockpit is a Zellij
38	  session).
39	- An MCP-capable agent CLI you want to drive. The `panopt up` launcher spawns
40	  [Claude Code](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) panes by
41	  default; any MCP client works for connecting by hand.
42	- Rust toolchain to build from source (no prebuilt binaries yet). A `flake.nix`
43	  is provided if you prefer Nix.
44	
45	Linux is the primary target. macOS works; the screenshot above is macOS.
46	
47	## Install
48	
49	```sh
50	git clone https://github.com/<your-fork>/panopt
51	cd panopt
52	just install        # or: cargo install --path crates/panopt && cargo install --path crates/panoptd
53	just plugin-release # build the Zellij sidebar plugin (wasm32-wasip1)
54	```
55	
56	`just install` puts the `panopt` launcher/CLI and the `panoptd` daemon in
57	`~/.cargo/bin`. The sidebar plugin builds to a separate `wasm32-wasip1` target
58	and is loaded by Zellij from the workspace; `panopt up` expects to find it at
59	`crates/panopt-zellij/target/wasm32-wasip1/release/panopt-zellij.wasm`. The
60	`just` recipes (`check`, `clippy`, `fmt`, `test`) sweep both the workspace and
61	the plugin so you do not have to remember the manifest-path/target dance.
62	
63	## Quick start
64	
65	From any project directory:
66	
67	```sh
68	panopt up
69	```
70	
71	That single command starts the daemon if it is not already running, opens a
72	Zellij cockpit session for the project (named after the project path so two
73	projects never share a session), mounts the five sidebar plugin panes, and
74	spawns a first Claude agent pane wired to PANopt. Re-running `panopt up` in a
75	project whose cockpit already exists just re-attaches.
76	
77	Inside the cockpit, each sidebar pane is the keyboard control surface for its
78	own kind of item. Focus the pane (click it or use Zellij's pane-focus binds),
79	then:
80	
81	| Key | What it does |
82	|---|---|
83	| `Up` / `Down`, `PageUp` / `PageDown`, `Home` / `End` | Move the cursor inside the focused pane. Arrowing previews the item in the content slot without moving focus off the sidebar. |
84	| `Enter` / left click | Activate the cursor row: swap its pane into the content slot. `Enter` also moves focus onto the content; a click leaves focus on the sidebar. |
85	| `a` | Spawn a new agent pane. |
86	| `c` | (Todos pane) Create a new todo and open the form on it. |
87	| `e` | (Todos pane) Open the form on the focused todo. |
88	| `n` | (Scratchpads pane) Create a new scratchpad and open the form on it. |
89	| `L` | Open the index list for this pane's kind in the content slot. |
90	| `Ctrl-Q` | Quit the cockpit (gated while items are open or in progress; you confirm in a dialog). |
91	
92	The content slot to the right of the sidebar is single-occupant: every todo,
93	scratchpad, agent, terminal, and command becomes a Zellij pane that swaps into
94	that slot when selected. Suppressed panes keep running hidden; the plugin
95	never closes them. The todo / scratchpad forms run in their own floating pane.
96	Inside a form: `Tab` cycles fields, `Ctrl-S` saves, `Ctrl-C` closes.
97	
98	## What lives where
99	
100	PANopt keeps two kinds of state:
101	
102	- A single SQLite database, shared across every project you use PANopt with.
103	  Default location: `~/.local/share/panopt/panopt.db` on Linux, the equivalent
104	  Application Support path on macOS. Override with `--db` on `panoptd`.
105	- A markdown projection inside each project, written to `.panopt/` under the
106	  project root. The directory contains a `.gitignore` of `*`, so it never
107	  enters your commits unless you ask it to.
108	
109	The projection mirrors the live state and is rewritten atomically on every
110	change:
111	
112	- `.panopt/todos.md` - a checklist index of every todo.
113	- `.panopt/todos/<id>.md` - one file per todo, with a frontmatter block of
114	  structured fields, the body, and the comment thread.
115	- `.panopt/scratchpads.md` - the scratchpad index.
116	- `.panopt/scratchpad/<id>.md` - one file per scratchpad.
117	- `.panopt/agents.md` - the agents currently connected.
118	- `.panopt/locks.md` - the advisory locks currently held.
119	- `.panopt/agent_tools.md` - the project's durable agent configurations: the
120	  config layer (name, command, cwd, tool type, enabled).
121	- `.panopt/processes.md` - the project's per-project process instances: agent,
122	  command, and terminal entries the cockpit launches and tracks.
123	
124	`agent_tools` and `processes` are the two-layer roster: one configuration can
125	back N running instances, and ids are drawn from the same per-project counter
126	so a `#N` reference resolves to exactly one row across todos, scratchpads,
127	agent tools, and processes.
128	
129	The projection is read-only today; edits go through the cockpit forms, the
130	MCP tools, or the CLI below.
131	
132	## Driving PANopt from the shell
133	
134	`panopt` is a small client of the daemon. Everything the cockpit does is also
135	available as a subcommand, so you can script it or use PANopt without
136	launching the cockpit at all.
137	
138	```sh
139	panopt todo list                    # every todo, one per line
140	panopt todo get 3                   # one todo in full
141	panopt todo create "wire the form"  # prints the new id
142	panopt todo set 3 --status in_progress --priority high --assignee alice
143	panopt todo set 3 --tags "ui, mcp"  # replaces the whole tag list
144	panopt todo done 3                  # mark complete
145	panopt todo rm 3                    # delete
146	panopt todo block 4 --by 3          # todo 4 is blocked by todo 3
147	panopt todo comment 3 "started" --as greg
148	panopt todo edit 3                  # interactive form on todo 3
149	panopt todo edit --new              # form on a fresh todo
150	
151	panopt agent-tool list              # durable agent configs (config layer)
152	panopt agent-tool add --name claude --command "claude" --tool-type claude
153	panopt agent-tool enable 7          # offer this tool in spawn UIs
154	panopt agent-tool disable 7         # hide without deleting
155	
156	panopt process list                 # agent/command/terminal instances
157	panopt process add --kind command --name build --command "cargo build"
158	
159	panopt agent [name]                 # spawn another agent pane in the cockpit
160	```
161	
162	Each invocation targets the current project (override with `--ws <path>`) and
163	auto-starts the daemon if needed.
164	
165	## Connecting your own agents
166	
167	The daemon speaks the standard Model Context Protocol over Streamable HTTP
168	and gates every request on a bearer token. The easiest way to point a
169	hand-launched Claude Code at it is `panopt agent-config`, which emits the
170	full `--mcp-config` JSON - stable agent id, friendly display name, token -
171	in one shot:
172	
173	```sh
174	claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name my-name)"
175	```
176	
177	The emitted URL has the shape
178	`http://<host>:<port>/mcp?ws=<abs-path>&agent=<id>&name=<friendly>&token=<token>`.
179	The `ws` parameter is the absolute project path; it scopes the connection to
180	that project. State is shared live across every agent connected with the
181	same `ws`. The `agent` parameter is a stable per-agent key (`name` is its
182	display label, applied as an implicit `identify` on first sight), so an
183	agent keeps one identity across reconnects.
184	
185	For any MCP client that lets you set request headers, `Authorization: Bearer
186	<token>` is preferred over the `?token=` query parameter; the token file is
187	at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` (mode 0600). The MCP surface includes
188	`todo_*`, `scratchpad_*`, `lock_*`, `agent_tool_*`, `process_*`, and the
189	agent registry (`identify`, `whoami`, `agent_list`). The full tool list is
190	in [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md).
191	
192	## Connecting an agent on another machine
193	
194	Run the daemon on one host (call it `A`, e.g. a workstation or NAS) and an
195	agent on another (`B`, e.g. a Mac with the Solo application, or a host with
196	USB-attached debug hardware). The agent connects to the daemon over the
197	LAN, joins the same coordination plane as local agents, and can be directed
198	to do work specific to `B`'s local resources.
199	
200	The agent host needs only the `panopt` binary and your agent CLI (e.g.
201	`claude`) - no `panoptd`, no Zellij.
202	
203	**On the daemon host (`A`):** bind to a public interface and extract the
204	token.
205	
206	```sh
207	panopt up --host 0.0.0.0
208	panopt token
209	```
210	
211	`panopt up --host 0.0.0.0` exposes the daemon on every interface (loopback
212	plus the LAN); pass a specific IP if you want to restrict it to one
213	interface. The bearer-token gate applies uniformly to loopback and remote
214	callers, so the daemon is never reachable without the token even when bound
215	to `0.0.0.0`.
216	
217	`panopt token` prints the token at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to stdout.
218	
219	**On the agent host (`B`):** point `agent-config` at the daemon host and
220	pass the token inline.
221	
222	```sh
223	TOKEN=$(ssh A panopt token)
224	claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config \
225	    --host A.local \
226	    --token $TOKEN \
227	    --name solo-mac)"
228	```
229	
230	`--host` is the daemon host's reachable address (an IP, hostname, or
231	mDNS name). `--token` accepts the value inline so `B` does not need a
232	local `~/.local/share/panopt/token` of its own. `--name` is the friendly
233	label other agents see in the registry; the stable `?agent=<id>` is
234	derived from `$USER-$HOSTNAME` by default, so `B`'s agent stays
235	distinguishable from cockpit-spawned panes on `A` across reconnects.
236	
237	The `_mcp-proxy` shim inside the emitted config reconnects across daemon
238	restarts, so the agent's MCP session on `B` survives `just up` on `A`.
239	
240	**Security note:** the daemon speaks plain HTTP, so the bearer token
241	travels in cleartext. This is fine on a trusted LAN; if `A` and `B` are
242	separated by an untrusted network, tunnel the connection through SSH
243	(`ssh -L 7600:localhost:7600 A` and use `--host 127.0.0.1` on `B`) or
244	WireGuard.
245	
246	## Running the daemon by hand
247	
248	`panopt up` and `panopt todo` both auto-start the daemon. If you would
249	rather run it yourself:
250	
251	```sh
252	panoptd --port 7600
253	```
254	
255	One daemon serves every project at once. The MCP endpoint is
256	`http://<host>:<port>/mcp`. Useful flags:
257	
258	- `--db <path>` - override the SQLite database location.
259	- `--host <addr>` - bind address (default `127.0.0.1`). Pass `0.0.0.0` to
260	  accept connections from other hosts; the bearer-token gate applies
261	  uniformly to local and remote callers.
262	- `--port <n>` - TCP port (default 7600).
263	
264	## Going deeper
265	
266	[DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) is the full design document: why PANopt is a daemon
267	rather than a fork of a terminal emulator, how the cockpit composes with
268	Zellij, the crate layout, and the MCP tool reference. Read it if you want to
269	understand the architecture or contribute.
270

The agent host needs only the `panopt` binary and your agent CLI (e.g.

**On the daemon host (`A`):** bind to a public interface and extract the
token.

```sh
panopt up --host 0.0.0.0
panopt token
```

`panopt up --host 0.0.0.0` exposes the daemon on every interface (loopback
plus the LAN); pass a specific IP if you want to restrict it to one
interface. The bearer-token gate applies uniformly to loopback and remote
callers, so the daemon is never reachable without the token even when bound
to `0.0.0.0`.

`panopt token` prints the token at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to stdout.

**On the agent host (`B`):** point `agent-config` at the daemon host and
pass the token inline.

```sh
TOKEN=$(ssh A panopt token)
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config \
    --host A.local \
    --token $TOKEN \
    --name solo-mac)"
```

`--host` is the daemon host's reachable address (an IP, hostname, or
mDNS name). `--token` accepts the value inline so `B` does not need a
local `~/.local/share/panopt/token` of its own. `--name` is the friendly
label other agents see in the registry; the stable `?agent=<id>` is
derived from `$USER-$HOSTNAME` by default, so `B`'s agent stays
distinguishable from cockpit-spawned panes on `A` across reconnects.

The `_mcp-proxy` shim inside the emitted config reconnects across daemon
restarts, so the agent's MCP session on `B` survives `just up` on `A`.

**Security note:** the daemon speaks plain HTTP, so the bearer token
travels in cleartext. This is fine on a trusted LAN; if `A` and `B` are
separated by an untrusted network, tunnel the connection through SSH
(`ssh -L 7600:localhost:7600 A` and use `--host 127.0.0.1` on `B`) or
WireGuard.

## Running the daemon by hand

`panopt up` and `panopt todo` both auto-start the daemon. If you would
rather run it yourself:

```sh
panoptd --port 7600
```

One daemon serves every project at once. The MCP endpoint is
`http://<host>:<port>/mcp`. Useful flags:

- `--db <path>` - override the SQLite database location.
- `--host <addr>` - bind address (default `127.0.0.1`). Pass `0.0.0.0` to
  accept connections from other hosts; the bearer-token gate applies
  uniformly to local and remote callers.
- `--port <n>` - TCP port (default 7600).

## Going deeper

[DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) is the full design document: why PANopt is a daemon
rather than a fork of a terminal emulator, how the cockpit composes with
Zellij, the crate layout, and the MCP tool reference. Read it if you want to
understand the architecture or contribute.

This is early alpha.  You can kinda do useful things with it right now, but it's

# PANopt

PANopt is the shared brain for a desk of terminal-based coding agents.

Run several AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, anything that speaks MCP) on
the same project at once, with a shared view of what's been done, what's
in flight, and what's coming next - all from one terminal window.

![The PANopt cockpit: five stacked sidebar panes on the left (todos, agents, terminals, commands, scratchpads), a content pane on the right](docs/cockpit.png)

## What you get

**A shared todo list.** Every agent sees the same todos. Assign them, set
priorities, add tags, leave comments, mark them blocked by other todos. You
edit them from the sidebar with a quick form; your agents create and update
them through MCP tools.

**Shared scratchpads.** Free-form notes that agents and humans both read and
write. Useful for "here's what I tried", "open questions", or a running log
of what an agent is doing - read live as the agent writes.

**Coordination, not collisions.** Advisory locks let agents (and you) claim
a todo or any other resource by name. The sidebar shows what's currently held
and by whom, so two agents won't quietly clobber each other.

**One terminal cockpit.** The cockpit is a [Zellij](https://zellij.dev)
session with five sidebar panes - todos, agents, terminals, commands,
scratchpads - and one big content pane on the right. Arrow through any list
and the content pane previews that item; press `Enter` to focus on it. Spawn
a new agent with `a`. Create a todo with `c`. Edit it with `e`.

**Multiple agents, one project.** Hit `a` in the agents pane to spawn another
agent in the cockpit. Each one shows up in the agent list with its own pane,
and they share todos, scratchpads, and locks - so they can hand work between
each other instead of stepping on each other.

**Remote agents.** Run the daemon on your workstation and connect an agent
from another machine on the LAN - a laptop, a Mac running Solo, a host with
USB-attached debug hardware. The remote agent joins the same coordination
plane as the local ones and can work on resources only it has access to.

**Your stuff stays yours.** Todos and scratchpads mirror to plain markdown
files under `.panopt/` in your project, alongside the SQLite database in
`~/.local/share/panopt/`. No cloud, no account, no lock-in. The `.panopt/`
directory is gitignored by default; check it in if you want the project's
todos to travel with the repo.

## Prerequisites

- [Zellij](https://zellij.dev) on your `PATH` (the cockpit is a Zellij
  session).
- An MCP-capable agent CLI. `panopt up` spawns
  [Claude Code](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) panes
  by default; any MCP client works for connecting by hand.
- Rust toolchain to build from source (no prebuilt binaries yet). A
  `flake.nix` is provided if you prefer Nix.

Linux is the primary target. macOS works; the screenshot above is macOS.

## Install

```sh
git clone https://github.com/<your-fork>/panopt
cd panopt
just install        # builds panopt + panoptd, installs to ~/.cargo/bin
just plugin-release # builds the Zellij sidebar plugin
```

## Quick start

From any project directory:

```sh
panopt up
```

That single command opens a cockpit for the project, mounts the sidebar
panes, and spawns a first Claude agent already wired in. Re-running
`panopt up` later just re-attaches.

### Keyboard

Focus a sidebar pane (click it or use Zellij's pane-focus binds), then:

| Key | What it does |
|---|---|
| `Up` / `Down`, `PageUp` / `PageDown`, `Home` / `End` | Move the cursor and preview the row in the content pane. |
| `Enter` / left click | Open the row in the content pane. `Enter` also moves focus there; a click leaves you on the sidebar. |
| `a` | Spawn a new agent. |
| `c` | (Todos) Create a new todo. |
| `e` | (Todos) Edit the focused todo. |
| `n` | (Scratchpads) Create a new scratchpad. |
| `L` | Open the index list for this kind of item. |
| `Ctrl-Q` | Quit the cockpit. |

Inside a form: `Tab` cycles fields, `Ctrl-S` saves, `Ctrl-C` closes.

## Driving PANopt from the shell

The `panopt` CLI can do everything the cockpit does, so you can script it
or work outside the cockpit entirely:

```sh
panopt todo list
panopt todo create "wire the form"
panopt todo set 3 --status in_progress --priority high --assignee alice
panopt todo done 3
panopt todo block 4 --by 3
panopt todo comment 3 "started" --as greg

panopt agent                # spawn another agent pane in the cockpit
panopt agent-tool list      # configured agent commands
panopt process list         # everything the cockpit is running
```

Each invocation targets the current project (override with `--ws <path>`)
and auto-starts the daemon if needed.

## Connecting agents you launched yourself

`panopt up` wires up the agents it spawns automatically. For an agent you
already started by hand - or one on another machine - use `panopt
agent-config` to print the connection details and pass them straight to
your agent CLI:

```sh
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name my-name)"
```

That session shows up in the agents list as `my-name` and shares the same
todos, scratchpads, and locks as the cockpit-spawned agents.

### From another machine

Run the daemon on one host (your workstation, a NAS) and an agent on
another (a Mac, a laptop, a host with USB debug hardware). The agent host
only needs the `panopt` binary and your agent CLI.

On the daemon host, bind to the LAN and print the token:

```sh
panopt up --host 0.0.0.0
panopt token
```

On the agent host, hand the token to your agent:

```sh
TOKEN=$(ssh workstation panopt token)
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config \
    --host workstation.local \
    --token $TOKEN \
    --name solo-mac)"
```

**Security note:** the daemon speaks plain HTTP, so the token travels in
cleartext. Fine on a trusted LAN; tunnel through SSH (`ssh -L
7600:localhost:7600 workstation` and use `--host 127.0.0.1`) or WireGuard
for anything less trusted.

## Going deeper

[DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) is the full design document - architecture, the MCP
tool reference, why PANopt is shaped the way it is. Read it if you want
to understand the internals or contribute.

This is early alpha.  You can kinda do useful things with it right now, but it's
lacking a bunch of functionality and is likely to see fundamental changes.

# PANopt

PANopt is the shared brain for a desk of terminal-based coding agents.

Run several AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, anything that speaks MCP) on
the same project at once, with a shared view of what's been done, what's
in flight, and what's coming next - all from one terminal window.

![The PANopt cockpit: five stacked sidebar panes on the left (todos, agents, terminals, commands, scratchpads), a content pane on the right](docs/cockpit.png)

## What you get

**A shared todo list.** Every agent sees the same todos. Assign them, set
priorities, add tags, leave comments, mark them blocked by other todos. You
edit them from the sidebar with a quick form; your agents create and update
them through MCP tools.

**Shared scratchpads.** Free-form notes that agents and humans both read and
write. Useful for "here's what I tried", "open questions", or a running log
of what an agent is doing - read live as the agent writes.

**Coordination, not collisions.** Advisory locks let agents (and you) claim
a todo or any other resource by name. The sidebar shows what's currently held
and by whom, so two agents won't quietly clobber each other.

**One terminal cockpit.** The cockpit is a [Zellij](https://zellij.dev)
session with five sidebar panes - todos, agents, terminals, commands,
scratchpads - and one big content pane on the right. Arrow through any list
and the content pane previews that item; press `Enter` to focus on it. Spawn
a new agent with `a`. Create a todo with `c`. Edit it with `e`.

**Multiple agents, one project.** Hit `a` in the agents pane to spawn another
agent in the cockpit. Each one shows up in the agent list with its own pane,
and they share todos, scratchpads, and locks - so they can hand work between
each other instead of stepping on each other.

**Remote agents.** Run the daemon on your workstation and connect an agent
from another machine on the LAN - a laptop, a Mac running Solo, a host with
USB-attached debug hardware. The remote agent joins the same coordination
plane as the local ones and can work on resources only it has access to.

**Your stuff stays yours.** Todos and scratchpads mirror to plain markdown
files under `.panopt/` in your project, alongside the SQLite database in
`~/.local/share/panopt/`. No cloud, no account, no lock-in. The `.panopt/`
directory is gitignored by default; check it in if you want the project's
todos to travel with the repo.

## Prerequisites

- [Zellij](https://zellij.dev) on your `PATH` (the cockpit is a Zellij
  session).
- An MCP-capable agent CLI. `panopt up` spawns
  [Claude Code](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) panes
  by default; any MCP client works for connecting by hand.
- Rust toolchain to build from source (no prebuilt binaries yet). A
  `flake.nix` is provided if you prefer Nix.

Linux is the primary target. macOS works; the screenshot above is macOS.

## Install

```sh
git clone https://github.com/<your-fork>/panopt
cd panopt
just install        # builds panopt + panoptd, installs to ~/.cargo/bin
just plugin-release # builds the Zellij sidebar plugin
```

## Quick start

From any project directory:

```sh
panopt up
```

That single command opens a cockpit for the project, mounts the sidebar
panes, and spawns a first Claude agent already wired in. Re-running
`panopt up` later just re-attaches.

### Keyboard

Focus a sidebar pane (click it or use Zellij's pane-focus binds), then:

| Key | What it does |
|---|---|
| `Up` / `Down`, `PageUp` / `PageDown`, `Home` / `End` | Move the cursor and preview the row in the content pane. |
| `Enter` / left click | Open the row in the content pane. `Enter` also moves focus there; a click leaves you on the sidebar. |
| `a` | Spawn a new agent. |
| `c` | (Todos) Create a new todo. |
| `e` | (Todos) Edit the focused todo. |
| `n` | (Scratchpads) Create a new scratchpad. |
| `L` | Open the index list for this kind of item. |
| `Ctrl-Q` | Quit the cockpit. |

Inside a form: `Tab` cycles fields, `Ctrl-S` saves, `Ctrl-C` closes.

## Driving PANopt from the shell

The `panopt` CLI can do everything the cockpit does, so you can script it
or work outside the cockpit entirely:

```sh
panopt todo list
panopt todo create "wire the form"
panopt todo set 3 --status in_progress --priority high --assignee alice
panopt todo done 3
panopt todo block 4 --by 3
panopt todo comment 3 "started" --as greg

panopt agent                # spawn another agent pane in the cockpit
panopt agent-tool list      # configured agent commands
panopt process list         # everything the cockpit is running
```

Each invocation targets the current project (override with `--ws <path>`)
and auto-starts the daemon if needed.

## Connecting agents you launched yourself

`panopt up` wires up the agents it spawns automatically. For an agent you
already started by hand - or one on another machine - use `panopt
agent-config` to print the connection details and pass them straight to
your agent CLI:

```sh
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name my-name)"
```

That session shows up in the agents list as `my-name` and shares the same
todos, scratchpads, and locks as the cockpit-spawned agents.

### From another machine

Run the daemon on one host (your workstation, a NAS) and an agent on
another (a Mac, a laptop, a host with USB debug hardware). The agent host
only needs the `panopt` binary and your agent CLI.

On the daemon host, bind to the LAN and print the token:

```sh
panopt up --host 0.0.0.0
panopt token
```

On the agent host, hand the token to your agent:

```sh
TOKEN=$(ssh workstation panopt token)
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config \
    --host workstation.local \
    --token $TOKEN \
    --name solo-mac)"
```

**Security note:** the daemon speaks plain HTTP, so the token travels in
cleartext. Fine on a trusted LAN; tunnel through SSH (`ssh -L
7600:localhost:7600 workstation` and use `--host 127.0.0.1`) or WireGuard
for anything less trusted.

## Going deeper

[DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) is the full design document - architecture, the MCP
tool reference, why PANopt is shaped the way it is. Read it if you want
to understand the internals or contribute.

cd panopt
just install        # builds panopt + panoptd, installs to ~/.cargo/bin
just plugin-release # builds the Zellij sidebar plugin
```

```

Focus a sidebar pane to drive it; each pane shows its own key hints in
the status bar.

or work outside the cockpit entirely:

panopt todo done 3
panopt todo block 4 --by 3

This is early alpha.  You can kinda do useful things with it right now, but it's
lacking a bunch of functionality and is likely to see fundamental changes.

# PANopt

PANopt is the shared brain for a desk of terminal-based coding agents.

Run several AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, anything that speaks MCP) on
the same project at once, with a shared view of what's been done, what's
in flight, and what's coming next - all from one terminal window.

![The PANopt cockpit: five stacked sidebar panes on the left (todos, agents, terminals, commands, scratchpads), a content pane on the right](docs/cockpit.png)

## What you get

**A shared todo list.** Every agent sees the same todos. Assign them, set
priorities, add tags, leave comments, mark them blocked by other todos. You
edit them from the sidebar with a quick form; your agents create and update
them through MCP tools.

**Shared scratchpads.** Free-form notes that agents and humans both read and
write. Useful for "here's what I tried", "open questions", or a running log
of what an agent is doing - read live as the agent writes.

**Coordination, not collisions.** Advisory locks let agents (and you) claim
a todo or any other resource by name. The sidebar shows what's currently held
and by whom, so two agents won't quietly clobber each other.

**One terminal cockpit.** The cockpit is a [Zellij](https://zellij.dev)
session with five sidebar panes - todos, agents, terminals, commands,
scratchpads - and one big content pane on the right. Arrow through any list
and the content pane previews that item; press `Enter` to focus on it. Spawn
a new agent with `a`. Create a todo with `c`. Edit it with `e`.

**Multiple agents, one project.** Hit `a` in the agents pane to spawn another
agent in the cockpit. Each one shows up in the agent list with its own pane,
and they share todos, scratchpads, and locks - so they can hand work between
each other instead of stepping on each other.

**Remote agents.** Run the daemon on your workstation and connect an agent
from another machine on the LAN - a laptop, a Mac running Solo, a host with
USB-attached debug hardware. The remote agent joins the same coordination
plane as the local ones and can work on resources only it has access to.
